Landmark Judgments in Indian Consumer Law

1. Indian Medical Association v. V.P. Shantha (1995)
   Core Principle: Medical services are covered under the Consumer Protection Act. Patients paying for treatment are "consumers". Only entirely free services at charitable or government hospitals are excluded.

2. National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Harsolia Motors (2023)
   Core Principle: A commercial entity can be a "consumer" if the goods/services purchased are not for the purpose of profit generation (resale).

purchased are not for the purpose of profit generation (resale). The test is the "purpose of purchase", not the identity of the buyer.

3. Spring Meadows Hospital v. Harjol Ahluwalia (1998)
   Core Principle: The definition of a "consumer" include beneficiaries of a service (e.g., a child being treated while parents pay). Hospitals are vicariously liable for negligence of their staff.

4. Ireo Grace Realtech Pvt. Ltd. v.

negligence of their staff.

4. Ireo Grace Realtech Pvt. Ltd. v. Abhishek Khanna (2021)
   Core Principle: One-sided and unreasonable clauses in Apartment Buyer's Agreements constitute "unfair trade practices". Developers cannot force buyers to accept unfair terms.

5. Maruti Suzuki India Ltd. v. Tapan Kumar Das (2021)
   Core Principle: Manufacturers are liable for product defects even if they have dealers. Liability cannot be shifted away from the manufacturer for manufacturing defects.

6.

hifted away from the manufacturer for manufacturing defects.

6. Nizam Institute of Medical Sciences v. Prasanth S. Dhananka (2009)
   Core Principle: Established high standards for "Damages" in medical negligence cases, emphasizing compensation that balances the loss of life/health with reasonable financial recovery.

7. New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v.

asonable financial recovery.

7. New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Hilli Multipurpose Cold Storage (2020)
   Core Principle: The 30+15 day time limit for the opposite party to file a written version in a consumer case is mandatory and CANNOT be extended by the forum.

PER JUSTICE RAJ SHEKHAR ATTRI, PRESIDENT The complainant (now
appellant) has assailed the order dated 01.09.2025 passed by the District Consumer
Disputes Redressal Commission-I, U.T., Chandigarh (in short the District
Commission), vide which the consumer complaint bearing no.31 of 2025 filed by him
was dismissed by it.
2. Before the District Commission, it was the case of the complainant/appellant that he was
maintaining a salary/savings account with the opposite party bank.

intaining a salary/savings account with the opposite party bank. On 20.06.2024, he visited the
ATM of the opposite party bank at Sector 17, Chandigarh and withdrew Rs.1,000/-. After the
transaction, his ATM card inadvertently remained inside the ATM machine. On 23.06.2024, the
complainant received SMS alerts showing five unauthorized transactions totaling Rs.39,000/- from
his account, which he had neither authorized nor executed.

with the Cyber Crime Cell and also informed the local police. Thereafter, on 25.06.2024, he
submitted a written complaint to the opposite party bank requesting reversal of the fraudulent
withdrawals.

disputed amount of Rs.39,000/-. The complainant contended that since the fraudulent withdrawals
occurred on 23.06.2024 and the same were reported on 25.06.2024 within two days and effectively
within three working days (excluding Sunday) his case falls under "Zero Liability" as per RBI
guidelines.

guidelines. Alleging deficiency in service and non-compliance with RBI directions, he filed a

occurred.

daily withdrawal limit of Rs.25,000/- had been exceeded. Consequently, the bank refunded

respondent, his ATM card remained in the machine.

unauthorized transactions amounting to Rs.39,000/- were debited from his account. He further
submitted that despite the fact that the opposite party itself admitted that the fraudulent
withdrawals took place on 23.06.2024 and the appellant reported the matter to the bank on
25.06.2024, yet, the District Commission dismissed the consumer complaint, while holding to the

9. The moot question, which falls for consideration is, as to whether , the appellant's case falls within
the category of "Zero Liability" or "Limited Liability" under the RBI Guidelines on unauthorized
electronic banking transactions, and whether the District Commission erred in dismissing the
complaint?

10. Before proceeding further, it is apt to reproduce the relevant section of the RBI guidelines qua
Limited Liability of a Customer , which reads as follows:-
"....."Limited Liability of a Customer
(a) Zero Liability of a Customer
6. A customer's entitlement to zero liability shall arise where the unauthorised
transaction occurs in the following events:
i.

the unauthorised
transaction occurs in the following events:
i. Contributory fraud/ negligence/ deficiency on the part of the bank (irrespective of
whether or not the transaction is reported by the customer).
ii.

whether or not the transaction is reported by the customer).
ii. Third party breach where the deficiency lies neither with the bank nor with the
customer but lies elsewhere in the system, and the customer notifies the bank within
three working days of receiving the communication from the bank regarding the
unauthorised transaction.
(b) Limited Liability of a Customer
7. A customer shall be liable for the loss occurring due to unauthorised transactions
in the following cases:
i.

ring due to unauthorised transactions
in the following cases:
i. In cases where the loss is due to negligence by a customer, such as where he has
shared the payment credentials, the customer will bear the entire loss until he reports
the unauthorised transaction to the bank. Any loss occurring after the reporting ofRamhet vs Punjab And Sind Bank Through Its Branch ... on 5 March, 2026

11. It may be stated here that it is an admitted position of this case that the fraudulent withdrawals
occurred on 23.06.2024; the appellant reported the matter to the opposite party bank on
25.06.2024 and that 23.06.2024 was followed by 24.06.2024 (Sunday). Thus, the reporting was
made within two days of the fraudulent transaction and effectively within three working days,
especially when excluding Sunday.

ely within three working days,
especially when excluding Sunday. As per the RBI Circular on "Customer Protection - Limiting
Liability of Customers in Unauthorised Electronic Banking Transactions", a customer is entitled to
zero liability where the deficiency lies neither with the bank nor with the customer but elsewhere in
the system; and the customer notifies the bank within three working days of receiving
communication regarding the unauthorized transaction.

eiving
communication regarding the unauthorized transaction.
12. In the present case, the respondent's own stand is that the fraudulent withdrawals occurred on
23.06.2024 and were reported on 25.06.2024. Therefore, the reporting was within the prescribed
period of three working days.

eporting was within the prescribed
period of three working days. The District Commission erred in computing negligence merely from
the fact that the ATM card was left in the machine, without appreciating that the liability framework
under RBI guidelines is triggered from the date of unauthorized transaction and reporting thereof.
13.

the date of unauthorized transaction and reporting thereof.
13. Furthermore, once the opposite party bank itself refunded Rs.14,000/- on the ground of excess
withdrawal beyond the daily limit, it impliedly acknowledged that unauthorized withdrawals had
indeed taken place and that internal safeguards had failed. In these circumstances, fastening the
entire loss upon the appellant is contrary to the spirit and mandate of the RBI guidelines.

ant is contrary to the spirit and mandate of the RBI guidelines. The
finding of the District Commission that the appellant alone was negligent and liable to bear the
entire loss is not sustainable in law and is hereby set aside.
14.

tire loss is not sustainable in law and is hereby set aside.
14. Keeping in view the above discussion, we are of the considered view that the impugned order
passed by the District Commission, being not based on the correct appreciation of evidence and law
on the point, suffers from illegality and perversity, warranting interference of this Commission.
15. In view of the aforesaid discussion, the impugned order passed by the District Commission is set
aside. This appeal stands partly allowed.

on the said amount from 23.06.2024 (the date of unauthorized withdrawal) onwards;

om 23.06.2024 (the date of unauthorized withdrawal) onwards;
ii. To pay Rs.20,000/- as compensation to the appellant/complainant for causing
him mental agony and physical harassment and also negligence and deficiency in
providing service. iii. To pay cost of litigation to the tune of Rs.10000/- to the
appellant/complainant. iv. This order be complied within a period of 30 days from
the date of receipt of a certified copy thereof, failing which, thereafter, the awarded
amounts shall carry interest @12% p.a.

Introduction The consumer protection laws establish a redressal mechanism whereby consumers
can claim monetary reliefs for defective goods, deficiency in service, and unfair trade practices.
Sections 14 and 22 of the Consumer Protection Act, 1986 empower the District,

nd 22 of the Consumer Protection Act, 1986 empower the District, State, and National
Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission to "to pay such amount as may be awarded by it as
compensation to the consumer for any loss or injury suffered by the consumer due to the negligence
of the opposite party". Such monetary reliefs i.e., compensation awarded would have to be based on
the facts and circumstances of each case, since the loss and injury suffered would vary. Given theSmt.

awarded in each case, it follows that there can be no uniformity in the award of compensation.
It is for the Consumer Forum to grant compensation to the extent it finds it reasonable, fair, and
proper in the facts and circumstances of a given case according to the established judicial standards
where the claimant can establish his charge.[1] These 'established judicial standards' have been laid
down in a plethora

Background and Facts of the Case The present case of Ghaziabad Development Authority v Balbir

Development Authority vs. Darsh Kumar, where it held that in cases of deficiency of service by
development authorities, the rate of interest awarded must be 18% per annum. Following this, the
Commission disposed of subsequent matters by its preceding award.

I. INTRODUCTION
‘Profit is sweet, even if it comes from deception. ’ – Sophocles
The consumer-trader relationship has often been fraught with such  
deception from the very inception of commerce. The digitisation of the 
marketplace has merely brought in new dimensions of deception, of which 
‘dark patterns’ is a rather diabolical example. The term signifies deceptive 
user interface design practices meant to manipulate consumer behaviour 
by the e-commerce platforms.

t to manipulate consumer behaviour 
by the e-commerce platforms. The Indian consumer law framework is not 
oblivious of such practices and their potential to cause consumer harm. Back 
in 2023, the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) had, in exercise 
of its powers conferred under Section 18 of the Consumer Protection Act  
2019 (CPA), notified the Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of 
Dark Patterns 2023 (2023 Guidelines).

vention and Regulation of 
Dark Patterns 2023 (2023 Guidelines). The decision delivered by the State 
Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (SCDRC), Chandigarh, on 20 
February 2024, in the matter of Ashwani Chawla v Flipkart Internet Pvt Ltd 
and Others  (Consumer Complaint No. CC/113/2023) assumes considerable 
significance in this context. It is one of the first judgments in Indian 
consumer law jurisprudence to explicitly apply the 2023 Guidelines.

manner in which these guidelines formalise the definition of dark patterns; 
seek to regulate not only advertisers and sellers, but also all platforms 
that systematically offer any good/service in India; and provide a detailed 
explanation of specified dark patterns and practices related thereto is 
unprecedented in the Indian consumer jurisprudence.

such practices among the judiciary, consumers, and traders and advertisers.

This is the main reason why the consumer court’s formal application of the

trade practices, and the jurisdiction of the SCDRC.

II. FACTS OF THE CASE
The facts of the case seem straightforward, yet they reveal many layers of 
consumer harm. The complainant, Ashwani Chawla, had bought a new 
mobile OnePlus handset through Flipkart, one of India’s leading e-commerce 2026 Ashwani Chawla v. Flipkart Internet Private Ltd 231

marketplace platforms. Despite this being a single transaction, two separate 
invoices were raised, one for the handset ( ₹40,941) and another referred 
to as ‘offer handling fee’ ( ₹49). Within a few weeks, the handset began to 
malfunction. At the brand’s exclusive service centre, the consumer learnt 
that the handset had been activated prior to his purchase. The service centre, 
without accepting responsibility, relegated the matter to the manufacturer, 
the seller, and the intermediary Flipkart.

to the manufacturer, 
the seller, and the intermediary Flipkart. They too did not provide any 
resolution, nor refund the money paid. As he had to buy a new phone at his 
own expense, he filed a consumer complaint under Section 47(1)(a)(ii) of 
CPA1 before the SCDRC, Chandigarh.

date of payment till the complainant’s receipt of such refund.

expenses, both the amounts having to be paid by the respondents.

The SCDRC also noted that the entire practice in display from the respondents’

VI. CONCLUSION
The Ashwani Chawla  case is likely to be considered a landmark judgment 
in Indian consumer rights jurisprudence for multiple reasons. Apart from 
the singular nature of the case in terms of the issues discussed above, it 
also brings interface design practices explicitly within the ambit of unfair 
contracts, which is likely to have future significance.

unfair 
contracts, which is likely to have future significance. Further, it extends the 
definition of ‘defect in goods’ to encompass not merely malfunctioning goods, 
but also goods that have been previously used contrary to the consumer’s 
reasonable expectations and the trader’s explicit representations.
As stated earlier, the explicit invocation of the 2023 Guidelines in this case 
represents a significant jurisprudential development.

rely upon general prohibitions on misleading advertisement, deficiency 
in service, and unfair trade practice to address manipulative platform 
behaviour.11 By recognising dark patterns as a distinct category of pernicious 
trader behaviour that can in turn trigger multiple statutory violations such 
as unfair contract, deficiency in service, unfair trade pra

this case provides consumer protection law in India with a foundation 
for addressing the systematic manipulative practices employed by digital 
platforms.

practices is also of considerable importance.12 Instead of viewing the double 
billing or the misrepresentation of product condition as isolated errors, 
the dark patterns framework as put forward by the court recognises them 
as deliberate design choices that collectively and cumulatively manipulate 
consumer behaviour and, in turn, subvert consumer autonomy.13 This

builder conflict 2.PDF.title: Smt. Amita Sharma vs M/S BCC Infrastructures Ltd (Consumer Refund Case)
builder conflict 2.PDF.summary: This is a legal judgment involving a homebuyer who sued a builder for failing to deliver a flat on time in Ghaziabad. The State Consumer Commission addressed the buyer's request for a refund after significant delays in the construction project.
key_takeaways: Homebuyers can file a complaint with the State Consumer Commission if a builder fails to provide possession of a property within the agreed timeframe.
Consumers are entitled to seek a refund of their deposited amount if the developer significantly delays the project.
The court recognizes 'deficiency in service' when a builder collects a large portion of the payment but fails to complete the construction.
Maintaining records of all payments, allotment letters, and correspondence with the builder is essential for a successful legal claim.

Make_My_Trip_India_Pvt_Ltd_vs_Damodar_Sh.title: Make My Trip India Pvt. Ltd. vs. Damodar Shriniwas Prabhu (2023) - Refund for Cancelled Flight Tickets
Make_My_Trip_India_Pvt_Ltd_vs_Damodar_Sh.summary: This case deals with a consumer's right to a full refund for international flight tickets booked through an online platform (Make My Trip) after the airline (Jet Airways) cancelled the flights. The court examined whether the booking agent or the airline is liable for the refund, especially when the airline faces bankruptcy.
key_sections: Section-3, Series-M, Part-II of DGCA CAR dated 22/05/2008
key_takeaways: Online travel agents often claim they are only 'facilitators' and that the airline is solely responsible for refunds.
According to DGCA guidelines, the obligation to refund ticket amounts for cancelled flights primarily rests with the concerned airline.
If an airline enters bankruptcy proceedings (NCLT), refund claims may be managed by an Interim Resolution Professional (IRP).
Consumers can seek relief through Consumer Commissions if they do not receive a full refund for services they paid for but did not receive.
